div.head: 19. Memorandum of ConversationSource: Department of State, Executive Secretariat, S/S-IRM Records, Lot 93D187, Secretary James A. Baker III’s Classified Papers, JAB Papers May 1989. Secret; Nodis. Drafted by Vershbow and Ridgway; cleared by Ross and Zoellick. The meeting took place in St. Catherine’s Hall, the Kremlin. The full memorandum of conversation is scheduled for publication in Foreign Relations, 1989–1992, vol. III, Soviet Union, Russia, and Post-Soviet States: High-Level Contacts. After this meeting, Baker flew to Brussels to brief NATO Foreign Ministers before returning to Washington the following day.
div.p: The Secretary’s May 10–11 Visit to Moscow: Plenary Meeting with Gorbachev

list.head: SUBJECTS
list.item: U.S.-Soviet Relations, Perestroyka, Nuclear and Space Talks, Short-Range Nuclear Forces, Conventional Arms Control

list.head: U.S.
list.item: James A. Baker III, Secretary of State
Jack F. Matlock, U.S. Ambassador to the USSR
Robert Gates, Deputy National Security Advisor to President
Robert Zoellick, Counselor, Department of State
Rozanne L. Ridgway, Assistant Secretary of State, EUR
Margaret DeB. Tutwiler, Assistant Secretary of State, PA
Dennis B. Ross, Director, Policy Planning Staff, State Dept.
Mikhail S. Gorbachev, General Secretary of the CPSU Central Committee, Chairman of the Presidium of the USSR Supreme Soviet
Eduard A. Shevardnadze, Minister of Foreign Affairs
Aleksandr A. Bessmertnykh, 1st Deputy Foreign Minister
Anatoliy F. Dobrynin, Advisor to Chairman Gorbachev
Marshal Sergey F. Akhromeyev, Advisor to Chairman Gorbachev
Yuriy F. Dubinin, USSR Ambassador to the United States
Anatoliy S. Chernyayev, Assistant to the General Secretary
Sergey P. Tarasenko, Assistant to the Foreign Minister

div.p:
Following his private meeting with the Secretary and Shevardnadze, Chairman Gorbachev welcomed the other members of the U.S. delegation. He noted that the Secretary’s visit was very timely. The Soviets believed that the U.S.-Soviet dialogue must continue, that it must be raised to a higher level and proceed toward real interaction and cooperation in all areas. Based on recent experience, he was optimistic about our countries’ ability to do so. We had begun cooperation and we saw that our
people hoped this cooperation would continue. Therefore it must continue. Gorbachev said he believed the only productive approach to our relationship was to base it on realities. It appeared this approach was shared by the President, judging by what Soviets had seen in the first months of the American Administration.
[Omitted here are discussions not related to START.]

The Secretary said it was noteworthy that the Soviets had decided to move toward openness in their political and economic spheres at the same time. It had always been our view that the two went hand-in-hand. In our perspective, one of the reasons why the Chinese were having problems was because they had not prepared the political ground for reform. In Poland perhaps the reverse was true.

The Secretary noted that he had told Shevardnadze at dinner the night beforeNo memorandum of conversation was found. that one way the Soviets could proceed now with price reform would be to quickly conclude agreements in START and CFE. The rubles saved could go into subsidies to ease the price reform. The Secretary had also pointed out that this would help us with our own budget deficit. Speaking as a politician who had spent the last 3½ years dealing with economic problems, the Secretary said he would be among those who would say that it was better to move early rather than later: this was because early people were better able to assign the consequent problems to previous Administrations. But one thing he had learned from 3½ years at the Treasury Department was that the political leadership of a country was far and away the best judge of what the traffic would bear in terms of economic reform. So therefore he would have to defer to Gorbachev’s judgment.

The Secretary noted that the General Secretary had said “stay the course.” That had been our own slogan for President Reagan’s campaign in 1984. We had been criticized for being insufficiently forward-looking, but the point was that the President had won reelection. So we understood what Gorbachev was saying and we respected it and we thanked him for sharing his views.
Gorbachev said the Soviets were 20 years late on price reform, so two or three years more wouldn’t hurt. He then asked what was the agreed date for the Nuclear and Space Talks.
Nuclear and Space Talks
The Secretary said that the U.S. was prepared to begin on June 12, 19 or 26. We had agreed to leave open the exact date within the second 10-day period in June.

Gorbachev said this was more or less what the Soviets were ready for. They had also been prepared to open the talks in May. He asked whether the Secretary had any comments on the negotiations themselves.

The Secretary said the U.S. had finished its foreign policy reviews but had not quite completed its reviews of each and every element of the various arms control negotiations. We expected to be ready to go on the agreed date and to maintain a considerable degree of continuity in our positions. In START we were prepared to use the joint draft text as a basis for discussion, but we reserved the right to make new proposals or to change the proposals of the previous Administration, and we assumed the Soviets also reserved such a right. But we expected a considerable degree of continuity.

Shevardnadze said that he and the Secretary had also agreed to resume the Nuclear Testing Talks and chemical weapons consultations about the same time as the Nuclear and Space Talks. There were also consultations planned on conventional forces. But the Soviets had received no support from the Secretary on their proposal to negotiate on tactical nuclear weapons.
Gorbachev said he would like to press that further.
The Secretary said Gorbachev should go ahead and press away.

Gorbachev said he would do so since the Secretary agreed. From what the Secretary had said, he wanted to emphasize that disarmament for development was a problem not only for the Third World, but also for the Soviet Union and the United States. It had taken the Soviets a while to find this out, but it was true. Future generations of Soviet and American analysts would want to see how our two countries, in pursuing the arms race, had marched almost to the abyss.

Returning to NST, Gorbachev said he welcomed what the Secretary had said about the U.S. approach to the negotiations on a 50-percent strategic arms reduction. We were not, however, beginning from scratch. The Soviets agreed that we had to build on what we had achieved in the past. Since the U.S. was in the last stages of developing its detailed position he wanted to emphasize three problems.

Generally speaking, Gorbachev explained, a 50-percent reduction was a tremendous undertaking. The INF experience was good and important, but it was not enough for this new treaty. We would need to work to build trust further. Any suspicion that either side was seeking advantage would harm the prospects for reaching agreement on the key issues. One example of this was the problem with air-launched cruise missiles. The U.S. proposal would give the U.S. the chance to have about 8,500 nuclear warheads, not 6,000. The Soviets had to ask themselves whether the U.S. was trying to outsmart the Soviet side.

A second issue was sea-launched cruise missiles, Gorbachev continued. The Soviets saw that the U.S. position had not changed, that the U.S. was arguing that a simple political declaration would be enough. Gorbachev asked whether the sides should do the same for strategic weapons: to propose a simple political declaration? That would clearly mean nothing. If we agreed on a START treaty with unlimited SLCM deployments, this would be an unacceptable situation.

Gorbachev said his third point was the link between START and the ABM Treaty as signed in 1972. This was a fundamental point for the Soviets. He understood that the Secretary did not want to engage now, but he wanted to say these things for the Secretary to think about.

Gorbachev noted that the U.S. reaction to his London proposal on fissionable material had been decidedly cool, but he would still like to propose a bilateral treaty on this subject, leading eventually to a ban on production. Gorbachev also wanted to stress that the Soviets were ready at any time to agree to a complete ban on nuclear testing. They welcomed the agreement by the U.S. to continue consultations on a chemical weapons ban. He suggested that the sides perhaps agree here on a joint statement. He knew that President Bush attached a lot of importance to CW. Such a statement could push the process forward. He asked whether the Secretary had any comment or whether he should proceed directly to conventional forces.

The Secretary said the U.S. did have an interest in CW and was prepared to continue the bilateral discussions, but we were not ready to issue a joint statement. This should probably await the resumption of our exchanges. The Secretary said that he had already spoken with Shevardnadze about SLCMs, as well as the resumption of talks on protocols to the PNET and TTBT with a view toward submitting the treaties for ratification. Regarding fissionable material, the Secretary said he was not in a position to be any warmer on this subject than before. Regarding ALCM counting rules, this was one of the tough issues that we would have to tackle in START if there were to be progress.

The Secretary said he was aware of the differences between our countries’ positions on the ABM Treaty, but we could not take them up on this occasion. The U.S. was concerned, for example, about the Krasnoyarsk radar, Gomel, and the whole issue of compliance. We wanted to take all these subjects up, but not at this meeting.
[Omitted here are discussions not related to START.]
